name: Goibniu
description: Goibniu is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, belonging to the Tuatha Dé Danann. He is revered as the divine smith and craftsman, possessing unparalleled skill in metalworking, particularly in the forging of weapons. His craftsmanship is not merely utilitarian; it is imbued with magical properties, rendering his creations exceptionally potent and often essential to the survival and success of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Goibniu's role extends beyond simply creating physical objects; he is also associated with the concept of immortality, as he brews ale that grants eternal youth and vitality to the gods. He is a central figure in the preparation for battles and feasts, ensuring the Tuatha Dé Danann are well-equipped and sustained. Goibniu's importance is highlighted in several key myths, underscoring his vital contribution to the divine society and his mastery over the transformative power of fire and metal. He represents the creative and protective aspects of craftsmanship, embodying the potential for both creation and destruction.
subtitle: The Smith God

keyMyths.title: The Forging of the Weapons
keyMyths.description: Goibniu, along with Luchtaine the carpenter and Credne the brazier, worked tirelessly to forge the weapons for the Tuatha Dé Danann before the Second Battle of Mag Tuired. Goibniu's spear, in particular, was renowned for its accuracy and lethality. This act was crucial to the Tuatha Dé Danann's preparation for war, demonstrating Goibniu's vital role in their success. His skills were unmatched, and his weapons were essential for victory against the Fir Bolg and later the Fomorians.
keyMyths.source: Lebor na hUidre (Book of the Dun Cow)

keyMyths.title: The Feast of Goibniu
keyMyths.description: Goibniu brewed a magical ale that granted immortality to the Tuatha Dé Danann. This ale was served at a great feast, where the gods consumed it to maintain their youth and vitality. This feast was crucial for the continued existence of the Tuatha Dé Danann, highlighting Goibniu's role in sustaining the divine society. The ale was not merely a beverage; it was a potent elixir of life, ensuring the gods' continued power and longevity.
keyMyths.source: Cath Maige Tuired (The Second Battle of Mag Tuired)

keyMyths.title: Goibniu's Vulnerability
keyMyths.description: During the Second Battle of Mag Tuired, Ruadan, son of Bres of the Fomorians, attempted to assassinate Goibniu. Ruadan asked Goibniu to forge him a spear, and then used it to attack him. Goibniu, despite being wounded, managed to kill Ruadan by throwing the same spear back at him. This myth highlights that even a god as skilled and powerful as Goibniu could be vulnerable, and also demonstrates his resilience and resourcefulness in the face of danger.
keyMyths.source: Cath Maige Tuired (The Second Battle of Mag Tuired)
